sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=36, col=36).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 20, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking at (36, 44) on the left-center separator changed the CENTER block from blue to red. The rule is: clicking on a separator changes the block to the RIGHT of the separator. The framed grid now has top row: blue, red, blue (matching top-left and top-right grids).
- Goal model: Make the framed grid match one of the unframed grids. Need to figure out which grid and how to change the left blocks.
- Action model: Clicking on separators changes the block to the right. Need to test if clicking on the charcoal frame changes the left block.
- Plan: Try clicking on the charcoal frame at (36, 36) to see if it changes the left block. Also try clicking on the center-right separator at (36, 52) to change the right block.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 19 of about 43 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking at (36, 36) on the charcoal frame changed the board. The left block in the top row changed from blue to red. Now the framed grid has:
- Top row: red, red, blue
Looking at the image, I can see the top-left block in the framed grid is now red, and the center block is also red (from the previous click).
So the rule is confirmed: clicking on a separator or frame changes the block to the RIGHT of it.
- Clicking on the charcoal frame (cols 36-37) changes the left block (cols 38-43).
- Clicking on the left-center separator (cols 44-45) changes the center block (cols 46-51).
- Clicking on the center-right separator (cols 52-53) changes the right block (cols 54-59).
Now the framed grid has:
- Top row: red, red, blue
- Middle row: blue, center, blue
- Bottom row: blue, blue, blue
I need to figure out which unframed grid to match. Let me check:
- Top-left: blue, red, blue / red, center, blue / blue, red, red
- Top-right: blue, red, blue / red, center, red / blue, red, blue
- Bottom-left: red, blue, blue / red, center, red / blue, blue, red
None of these have a top row of red, red, blue. So I've made a mistake by clicking on the charcoal frame. I need to undo this change.
But wait, there's no undo action. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, maybe clicking on the separator again toggles the block back. So clicking on the left-center separator again would change the center block from red to blue.
Let me try clicking on the left-center separator at (36, 44) again to toggle the center block back to blue.
Then I can try clicking on the center-right separator at (36, 52) to change the right block from blue to red.
This would give me: red, blue, red for the top row, which still doesn't match any unframed grid.
Hmm, let me think about this more carefully. The unframed grids have:
- Top-left top row: blue, red, blue
- Top-right top row: blue, red, blue
- Bottom-left top row: red, blue, blue
None of these have red, red, blue or red, blue, red. So maybe the goal is NOT to match one of the unframed grids.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the current state. After clicking at (36, 36), the left block changed from blue to red. So the top row is now: red, red, blue.
But I previously clicked at (36, 44) which changed the center block from blue to red. So the top row was: blue, red, blue before clicking at (36, 36).
Now the top row is: red, red, blue.
If I click at (36, 44) again, the center block should toggle from red to blue. So the top row would be: red, blue, blue, which matches the bottom-left grid's top row!
Let me try this.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking at (36, 36) on the charcoal frame changed the LEFT block from blue to red. The framed grid top row is now: red, red, blue. The rule is: clicking on a separator/frame toggles the block to the RIGHT of it.
Goal model: Make the framed grid match the bottom-left grid (red, blue, blue / red, center, red / blue, blue, red).
Action model: Clicking toggles the block to the right of the clicked separator/frame. Clicking again toggles it back.
Plan: Click at (36, 44) to toggle the center block back to blue, giving top row: red, blue, blue (matching bottom-left). Then continue to match the middle and bottom rows.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click at (36, 44) to toggle the center block back to blue
# This should give top row: red, blue, blue (matching bottom-left)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 44}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
